cue: Insert two keys in order, then read the Map's entries back as an array.
note: map.entries() yields [key, value] pairs in the order keys were inserted; spreading them into an array gives a plain, typeable view of the whole Map.

cue: Ask what a Map returns for a key that was never set.
snippet:
const m = new Map([['a', 1]]);
m.get('z');

cue: Trace how a plain object coerces both a number and a string key.
Compare the key type you stored against the key type you query.
Accumulate a running hash across the characters left to right.
prompt: Write `hashStr(s, mod)` that combines the characters of `s` into an integer in the range `[0, mod)` using `h = h * 31 + charCode` accumulated left to right, taking `% mod`.
note: Each character has a numeric code (charCodeAt); multiplying the running total by 31 makes position matter. Collisions still happen: at size 8 both 'tea' and 'eat' hash to bucket 0, which is exactly why each bucket holds a list you scan by full key.

concept: method reads and writes this, returns this to chain
cue: A method sees the instance through this; returning this lets the next call continue on the same object.
note: this is the receiver of the call; returning it enables method chaining like b.add(1).add(2).

cue: Scan the target bucket for a matching key before appending a new pair.
prompt: Complete `set(key, val)` on `MyMap`: if `key` is already present, update its value; otherwise add it. Return `this`.

cue: Walk the key's bucket comparing each stored key.
prompt: Complete `get(key)` on `MyMap`: return the value stored for `key`, or `undefined` if absent.

cue: Reuse the bucket scan but report a boolean instead of the value.
prompt: Complete `has(key)` on `MyMap`: return `true` if `key` is present, else `false`.

concept: || supplies a fallback for a missing key
cue: A key that was never set reads undefined; the fallback turns that into a starting number.
snippet:
const m = new Map();
m.get('z') || 0;
note: Reading an absent key gives undefined, which is falsy, so `|| 0` yields 0. This is the trick a frequency counter uses to start each new key at zero.

cue: Walk the string and track each character's running count in the Map.
Count every character, then read the entries in the order keys arrived.
Note how a stored count of zero behaves under a falsy fallback.
note: || falls through on any falsy value, so a real stored 0 gets clobbered; ?? only falls through on null/undefined.

cue: Tally characters into a plain object as you scan.
for...in iterates the keys of an object, not its values.
note: for...in gives an object's keys (always strings); for...of iterates the values of an array or other iterable. Reach for for...in when your hash map is a plain object.

cue: Loop the string and bump each character's tally.
prompt: Write `charFreq(s)` that returns an object mapping each character of `s` to its count.

concept: frequency counter on the hand-built map
cue: Feed each item through the map's own set/get to accumulate counts.
prompt: Using the finished `MyMap`, write `freqCount(items)` that returns a `MyMap` mapping each item to how many times it appears.

cue: Count first, then scan positions for the earliest character with count one.
Two passes: build counts, then find the first single.
prompt: Write `firstUniqChar(s)` that returns the index of the first character occurring exactly once, or `-1` if there is none.
mapsTo: LC 387 · First Unique Character in a String

cue: Track the values you have seen; for each new one, look for the earlier value that adds up to target.
For each value, check whether the value it needs (target minus it) was already seen, before recording the current value.
prompt: Write `twoSum(nums, target)` that returns `[i, j]` of two distinct indices whose values sum to `target`, or `null` if none exist.
note: Storing seen values as you go replaces the nested double loop with a single pass; each map lookup is one constant-time step (O(1)).

cue: Bucket each number by whether it is even or odd, appending into the matching list.
Key each word by its sorted letters and append into that group.
prompt: Write `groupAnagrams(words)` that returns an array of groups, each group holding the words that are anagrams of each other. Preserve the order in which each group's key was first seen, and within a group the input order.

concept: value to index, last write wins
cue: Trace which index survives when a value repeats.
Store an index only the first time you meet each value.
prompt: Write `buildIndex(nums)` that returns a `Map` from each value to the index of its FIRST occurrence.

cue: Compare the deduped size against the original length.
snippet:
const arr = [1, 2, 2, 3];
new Set(arr).size !== arr.length;

cue: A Set drops later repeats; spread what survives into an array.
snippet:
const arr = [3, 1, 2, 3, 1];
[...new Set(arr)];

concept: order-preserving dedup with a seen set
cue: Track seen values in an object and emit each the first time only.
prompt: Write `dedup(arr)` that returns a new array with duplicates removed, keeping the first occurrence of each value in its original order. Do it by hand, without a Set.

cue: Count first, then order the entries by their count before taking a slice.
Build a frequency map, then order its entries by count and take the leaders.
prompt: Write `topKFrequent(nums, k)` that returns the `k` values that appear most often, most frequent first.
note: This sorts every entry (O(n log n)); the optimal LC 347 solution uses a heap or a bucket sort to pull the top k in O(n).

cue: Build an array where entry i holds the sum of every element before index i.
note: With prefix sums, the sum of the slice nums[i..j] equals pref[j + 1] - pref[i]; the subarray-sum counter is built on that fact.

concept: prefix-sum counts via a seen map
cue: Carry a running sum; a subarray ends here for every earlier prefix equal to sum minus k.
note: seen maps each running prefix sum to how many times it occurred; seeding {0: 1} counts subarrays that start at index 0.

cue: Keep a map of prefix-sum frequencies and add in every match as you scan.
prompt: Write `subarraySum(nums, k)` that returns how many contiguous subarrays sum to `k`. Values may be negative.

concept: count runs only from sequence starts
cue: Put everything in a set; only begin counting a run at a value whose predecessor is absent.
note: Skipping any x whose x-1 is present means each run is walked once from its start, so the whole scan is O(n), not O(n log n).

cue: Load a set, then from each run-start count upward while the next value is present.
prompt: Write `longestConsecutive(nums)` that returns the length of the longest run of consecutive integers in `nums`, in O(n) time (no sorting). The values need not be adjacent in the array.
